The Fund will blend public and private finance to focus on a new asset class comprised of small and mid-size infrastructure projects with high positivie climate impacts related to Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) and Nature-based Solutions (NbS).

• The programme takes a bottom-up, demand led approach to addressing the funding gap at the sub-national level for small and mid-sized projects, lacking bankability and funding sources.
• The programme will serve as the first fund in the world to make use of a certification and reporting methodology in all investment activities in an individual project level.

1. The objective of this project is to develop climate resilient and sustainable
fisheries in Saint Lucia for all fish landing sites around the island and shift
the sector towards climate resilient pathway. The project is structured around
three components presented below.


Component 1: Enhancement of the fisheries information management system. This
component addresses the information barriers by disseminating and enhancing
proven technologies and practices such as the system of data collection and
analysis implemented under the readiness project, currently being prepared, and
capacity building, including trainings on safety at sea being implemented in
other Caribbean countries. The systems of data collection and analysis will be
strengthened to include critical fisheries and aquaculture and expand
socio-economic and other relevant climate change adaptation supporting
information to enable more informed adaption planning, implementation and
monitoring in the fisheries sector. This technology innovation is complemented
with a co-management system for data collection involving fishing communities
with the use of mobile applications. In component 1, GCF finances technical
consultancies, the purchase of equipment to enhance the information management
system in the fisheries sector and trainings. All activities in Component 1 are
aligned with the Fisheries SASAP concept note 3- “Increasing the capacity of
fishers and other actors to manage climate risks through improved data
management and Early Warning Systems (EWS) “.

Sub-component 1.1[1]. Enhance theFisheries Statistics System based on the
documentedproven integrated registries and advanced reporting platform
utilisation.This sub-component will enhance and scale-up data collection and
analysis systems on climate, productivity, livelihoods and other information
that would support fishers and the government in planning, implementing and
monitoring climate resilience measures in the fisheries sector. In sub-component
1.1, the project will strengthen and expand the localized Fisheries Statistics
System from a limited number of landing sites in capture fisheries in Saint
Lucia to a web-based and centralized information system for the whole
country/fisheries. . Through scaling up synergistic project interventions in
Saint Lucia and taking reference from information system that has been
successfully tested and is being deployed in the small-scale fisheries context
in Trinidad and Tobago, the project will strengthen access to climate
information for climate-resilient fisheries in the Caribbean It will also
mainstream data on fish aggregating devices (FAD) management, aquaculture and
further socio-economic indicators including sex and age disaggregated data;
training in data collection and management systems and; the validation and
maintenance of the system with review of the statistics generated.Given the
wider scope (nationwide system) of this initiative, linkages with the national
statistical system will be made for robustness and sustainability









Sub-component 1.2.Enhancment of technical capacities of Saint Lucia Fisheries
Department staff, fishers and fisherworkers in the use and analysis of gender
sensitive data, climate-related information and statistics.Sub-component 1.2
focuses on reinforcing the technical capacities to improve access, collect and
process climate-related information to improve the climate resilience of the
fisheries sector in Saint Lucia. Lessons leant and experiences will be build on
similar interventions at limited scale.

In this sub-component, staff members from the Fisheries Department will benefit
from capacity building on fisheries socio-economic study and indicators[2];
collection of aquaculture data and statistical analysis; data analysis,
including stock assessment and; reporting for national institutions, and
regional and international organizations. Among others, the essential need of
involving the primary fisheries resource users (and primarily affected by
climate change impacts) in the data collection will be strengthened. This is
justified by the fact that an important feature in this project is to enhance
the involvement of fishers, fisherworkers and fishing communities in the data
collection and feeding back to them the processed climate-related information.
The regional dimension in the data reporting will also be addressed, given that
Saint Lucia is an active member of several (sub) regional economic integration
and resource management organizations that require data for sound decision
making.




Sub-component 1.3. Strengthening the instruments to monitor impact of climate
change on fisheries sector.Sub-component 1.3 supports the development of
reporting tools for improving the information management system of the fisheries
sector and the adoption of climate resilient measures. This will be achieved
through the systematization and extension of the evaluation of fisheries sector
value to all fisheries and aquaculture; the upgrade and implementation of the
advanced dashboards and reporting tools, and training of the Department of
Fisheries staff in the use of these dashboards. This sub-component is aligned to
the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030)[3] as it
contributes to understanding disaster risk; strengthening disaster risk
governance to manage disaster risk; investing in disaster reduction for
resilience and; enhancing disaster preparedness for effective response.

Sub-component 1.4. Production and dissemination of gender sensitive
resilient-strengthening information for climate-resilient fisheries and
dissemination of lessons learned.Sub-component 1.4 aim at ensuring an efficient
interface between science and policy making, settin-up mechanisms for
information to be able to influence policy-making. It finances the production
and dissemination of relevant communication materials for awareness raising and
dissemination of lessons learned from the implementation of the project
activities. Activities include outreach workshops in the main fishers’
communities with all stakeholders to raise awareness of the importance of the
data collection; develop communication materials on a yearly basis to inform the
decision-makers and fisheries planners, private investors and fisherfolk and
general public and; explore ways to properly utilize data and information for
evidence-based policy-making and sector management. Activities include develop
promotional material on defined topics like some other projects components (Use
of Fisheries Aggregating Devices, awareness of the value of the overall sector
value; the nutritional benefits of fish, safety at sea); streamline and
institutionalize instruments for fisher communities to share their knowledge
with the Department of Fisheries and; training of fisherfolks for the use of
mobile applications for a co-management system of data collection.

Sub-component 1.5. Knowledge transfer and capacity building on safety at sea to
inform fisherfolks on extreme weather events.Finally, sub-component 1.5 will
provide better and updated climate information for fishers and train fishers to
be able to respond to safety at sea information, building on smartphone and
weather products that have been developed and successfully implemented in
previous initiatives (see paragraph 12 and Annex 3). These priorities have been
identified in Saint Lucia’s NAP, and were included in the Fisheries SASAP
concept note 3- Increasing the capacity of fishers and other actors to manage
climate risks through improved data management and Early Warning Systems (EWS)”.
The activities include enhancing systems for accessing communications on climate
forecasts safety at sea including additional hardware (radio transmitters) and
operations; deliver formalized training and certification in understanding and
responding to forecasts/safety at sea for fishers, and improve reporting
systems, monitoring and climatic data collection from fishers.




Component 2. Implemented priority interventions to enhance the fisheries sector
resilience to climate change.This component addresses each of the priority risks
identified in Section B1 in turn, focusing on the assessment of gaps and
solutions in the fisheries value chain and the identification and implementation
of interventions to increase resilience to climate change by addressing the
technical, information, financingand gender (special focus will be given on
gender maintrainming, ensuring greater participation and benefit of women and
youth in the sector) barriers. In component 2, GCF will finance technical
consultancies, technical assistance, workshops and the purchase of equipment
(such as radio transmitters and solar dryers). Co-finance from the Department of
Fisheries will support the project with technical assistance, rooms for
workshops and field schools as well as resources for travels to the communities.
Activities will be implemented at the community and cooperatives level.

Sub-component 2.1. Implement climate-resilient aquaculture and resilient coastal
livelihood diversification.This sub-component will involve the assessment of
main gaps in the fisheries value chain as a whole and identification and
implementation of solutions to increase resilience to climate change in the
sector, involving all relevant stakeholders (including the
`fisherfolk`communities, the government and private sector enterprises of the
fisheries sector). This subcomponent aim at developing climate-resilient coastal
aquaculture, supporting existing aquaculture farmers to address the changing
climate, as well as developing the sector to provide more resilient livelihoods
for coastal communities affected by climate change. It will include a range of
activities for capacity building and access to resources (for the implementation
of climate resilient practices) through field schools to increase climate
resilience, with a priority given to women and youth. These are included in the
NAP and Fisheries SASAP concept note 5- “Strengthening aquaculture and
mariculture farming through diversification and business opportunities”. Under
CC4FISH an Aquaculture Development and Management Plan was developed in
consultation with stakeholders, to guide the development of aquaculture for the
next five years. The activities include: assessing the fisheries value chain to
identify the main gaps and possible solutions to increase the resilience to
climate change; the development ofclimate resilient aquaculture field schools
and curriculum activities, establishing demonstration sites in all fishing
communities, including equipment for training; awareness raising and training,
including it in national vocational standards for aquaculture at the aquaculture
field schools; demonstration of solar dryers, targeting two highly vulnerable
coastal communities; technical assistance for business plans and value chain
development for a climate-resilient fisheries and aquaculture sector engaging
relevant stakeholders involved in the sector (`fisherfolk`communities, the
government and private sector enterprises); the development of a special
planning framework to ensure that aquaculture operations stay within the
carrying capacity of surrounding ecosystems, and the implementation of
recirculating aquaculture systems such as aquaponics and the development and
promotion of more efficient on farm water management systems.

Sub-component 2.2. Designing Climate Resilient Fisheries Infrastructure.There is
a need to climate proof existing fisheries infrastructure to the changing
climate, and ensure new upgrades and sites are climate resilient. This component
will provide technical assistance to review the level of risk for current
infrastructure and provide new designs for current and future infrastructure for
rolling renewal. These activities are included in the Fisheries SASAP concept
note 6- “Upgrading fisheries facilities to be more resilient to the impacts of
climate change” (but this project does not include actual infrastructure
construction). The activities include: climate resilient design for fishery
facilities, including design standards, building codes and engineering plans;
technical assistance to design a climate resilient upgrade of Castries fish
landing site, and technical assistance to develop a business model to raise
revenues for implementation of designs, as well as maintenance and future
upgrade.

Component 3. Strengthened climate-resilient and gender sensitive fisheries
policy and planning.This component will strengthen the institutional capacity
and regulatory framework for mainstreaming climate change and achieve effective
planning and responsive fisheries policy implementation. In component 3, GCF
will finance technical consultancies, trainings and workshops while the
Department of Fisheries will co-finance with technical assistance and travel
expenses.

Sub-component 3.1. Strengthening climate mainstreaming capacity and integration
in fisheries and aquaculture.This sub-component will strengthen institutional
capacity to prepare for climate change in the fisheries and aquaculture sector
and balance food security, adaptation and mitigation goals. It will support
mainstreaming of climate resilience fisheries and aquaculture into the policy,
regulatory and legal framework in Saint Lucia, to strengthen climate resilient
approaches in future sector development planning. Considering the barriers
identified above, special focus will be given on gender maintrainming, ensuring
greater participation and benefit of women and youth in the sector. This will
provide a framework to ensure the uptake of component 2 interventions in
fisheries policies and plans, and provide a clear exit strategy for the project.
Activities include: the production of training materials for integrating climate
resilience into extension services, vocational training standards and quality
control standards and; awareness raising and training using material produced in
sub-component 1.4 for integrating climate change into extension services,
vocational training standards and quality control standards.

Sub-component 3.2.Nationalmonitoring and adaptive learning as part of a Coastal
Adaptation Blueprint.This sub-component will support national monitoring and
evaluation and use of this in a learning-based adaptive management approach,
including the establishment or strengthening of an appropriate mechanism for
participatory monitoring and evaluation to facilitate learning by doing. It
implements the coastal adaptation blueprint in theFisheries SASAP (concept note
9- “Coastal Adaptation Blueprint for Saint Lucia”)to ensure climate change
evidence is integrated into future fisheries policy and planning. Activities
include: technical assistance to integrate learning and use information in
fisheries policy implementation planning, as part of adaptive mangagement to
help address changing risks and; technical assistance in the monitoring and
evaluation of the FAD management plan and connected socio-economic information.

2. A theory of change has been produced for the project, presented in Annex 4.

The anticipatedimpactis a climate resilient sector in Saint Lucia. The outcomes
and outputs are:

Outcome 1) Stakeholders have access to adequate information to improve
resilience to climate change in the fisheries sector:

Output 1. Information management system for adaptation measures planning,
implementing and monitoring is improved:

· Improve information management system through equipment, training and
knowledge sharing.

· Improve safety at sea through equipment, user awareness and training.




Outcome 2) Resilience to climate change is strengthened in fisheries sector:

Output 2. Priority climate resilient interventions to address major climate
threats are implemented:

· Gaps and solutions to increase resilience in the fisheries value chain
identified with stakeholders involved in the sector.

· Climate resilient practices taken up by fishers, through capacity building,
knowledge and demonstration and pressure on ecosystems reduced,

· Disseminate climate resilient coastal aquaculture and resilient livelihoods
through demonstration awareness and vocational standards,

· Design resilient coastal infrastructure and develop sustainable business
models.




Outcome 3) Climate change is mainstreamed into fisheries policy and planning.

Output 3. Increased capacity for climate resilient planning and development:

· Institutional strengthening for climate responsive planning and mainstreaming,

· Strengthen staff technical capacity,

· Integrate climate resilient curriculum and activities in extension services,

· Use and link information and learning to inform future fisheries policy and
plans.




3. The components outlined in this concept note have all undergone recent
analysis and activities in Saint Lucia, funded under previous projects (as
outlined in Annex 3). These have identified the potential for scaling up and
transformative impacts (see also Annex 3), in specific high risk areas (safety
at sea, resilient fisheries, and climate resilient aquaculture) and for
mainstreaming climate change adaptation into fisheries planning and policy. All
of the interventions have high benefit to cost ratios (See section B3 on
efficiency and effectiveness).




4. The Accredited Entity is the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO). FAO has
existing experience as an AE for GCF funded projects. FAO is already undertaking
climate change adaptation activities in fisheries in the Eastern Caribbean and
specifically in Saint Lucia. Success is further demonstrated from other
countries (e.g. Grenada, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Trinidad and Tobago) which can
be replicated in Saint Lucia. For example, in Grenada, the Climate Change
Adaptation of the Eastern Caribbean Fisheries Sector Project (CC4FISH) is
developing and implementing fisheries and emergency response training at the
community level in order to improve disaster preparedness as well as improve
recording of damages and losses of the fisheries sector in Post-Disaster Damage
and Needs assessment. In Saint Kitts and Nevis, there has been implementation of
aquaponics school systems which can improve linkages with youth and agriculture,
and support learning and knowledge exchange as well as basic fishers training to
improve resilience to climate change impacts. In Grenada, St. Kitts and Nevis
and Trinidad and Tobago there has been extensive training in ICT for fisherfolk
to improve safety at sea e.g training in use of VHF radios, GPS use and enhanced
use of cellphones. FAO has successfully developed and implemented Fisheries
Statistics and Management Information Systems - FISMIS (The Bahamas, Trinidad
and Tobago; in progress for Suriname and Grenada) with a the support of the
Fisheries Agency Japan and regional projects such as CC4FISH, REBYC II LAC and
CLME+. The new FISMIS platform called Calipseo is instrumental to provide timely
and reliable fisheries data and information as evidences of climate change
impact on fisheries and to inform mitigation actions and policies. It also
addresses the complexity of assessing small-scale fisheries sector. FAO has a
team of statisticians and FISMIS experts with long standing experience in the
region. The FAO has a dedicated high-quality project management unit, with
expertise in all the relevant areas for project execution, including M&E, legal,
administrative and finance, and effective project management. This unit has been
operational for many years and has a demonstrated track record. FAO will also
act as the project manager, including procurement activities, and thus carry out
the executing role for the project.



Implementation arrangements

5. A project management unit (PMU) will be established within the Department of
Fisheries of the Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries, Physical Planning in Saint
Lucia, which is the project Executing Entity. The project will be overseen by a
Steering Committee. This will serve as the decision-making body and will review
progress and evaluations, facilitate implementation and provide guidance. It
will involve Government representatives from Ministries, Authorities and
Agencies, including the NDA, the Department of Statistics and the UNFCCC Climate
Focal Point. It will include representatives from private sector including
cooperatives and small processing enterprises (described in annex 2),
non-governmental and community-based organizations such as St. Lucia Fish
Marketing Corporation, Lucian Blue Oceans, the Soufriere Marine Management
Association and the St Lucia Fisherfolk Organization (SLFFO), etc. .) gender and
community-based organizations and non-profit technical organizations like the
Caribbean Natural Resources Institute (CANARI), UWI (CERMES and CIRP). The
committee will be responsible for ensuring there is a strong gender balance and
participation from identified vulnerable groups throughout the project.




6. This project builds on previous and ongoing projects (see section B.1 and
Annex 3). It uses a professional international accredited entity as well as
existing institutional arrangements and there is a high confidence on the
project delivery and success. For these reasons, the risks to the implementation
and execution are considered low. The FAO has existing protocols and guidance
for financial and operational risk and these will be applied in the project.




7. The project will undertake M&E to ensure the project undertakes routine and
concurrent monitoring, interim and final evaluations, and annual reports. As an
international AE, the FAO will ensure all M&E is undertaken and reported using
international best practice standards, including the use of independent
evaluation teams.
Accelerating the transformational shift to a low-carbon economy in the Republic of Mauritius

1.    The project will strengthen adaptive capacities and enhance the
livelihoods’ resilience of women smallholder farmer communities by disseminating
a ‘package’ of integrated, gender-transformative solutions identified on the
basis of the possible intervention priorities along with strategic approaches
described in the pre feasibility study[1]
[https://wfp.sharepoint.com/sites/GCFCotedIvoire/Shared%20Documents/General/Concept%20Note%20development/Concept%20Note%20&%20attachments/4.%20Fourth%20submission%20to%20the%20GCF%20(December%202021)/GCF%20SAP-concept-note_Cote%20d'Ivoire_WFP_Revised_November2021_final.docx#_ftn1].
It is structured under three intertwined components that tackle the main climate
change hazards affecting Poro Region (droughts, interannual rainfall variability
and heavy floods and winds) and the above-mentioned adaptation barriers. The
gender-transformative nature of this project revolves around the highly
significant participation, awareness, strengthening of skills, leadership and
financial capacities of women for all project activities from community to
household levels. Households headed by women and women-led FOs will be involved
in climate empowerment actions key to the project scope:

·      Awareness-raising about climate change hazards and impacts to local
livelihoods

·      Participatory mapping of selected climate-resilient measures to address
adaptation barriers

·      Training and capacity-building of adapted livelihoods options, climate
risk products and services for small-scale food production,

·      Implementation and dissemination of a set of integrated adaptive measures
including nature-based solutions and climate-risk management tools (C1 and C2)

·      Empowerment of FOs through financial literacy, organizational management,
access to savings and loans (C3)

·      Linkages with markets and improved sales of staple crops and vegetables
while fostering climate-proofing investments (C3)

 

2.    The project seeks to roll out a participatory process calling for
evidence-based and results-driven interventions. These will demonstrate that,
when key root causes of inequalities are addressed, both men and women can bring
about positive change in their communities. The project will create an enabling
environment to bring to the front of the discussions with local stakeholders the
importance of ensuring women have equal access to improved agricultural inputs
and means of production. Women are usually given access to less productive
marginal lands to carry out their crop production. To address this inequality,
the project will engage men and youth in land-reclamation and land-restorations
and erosion control activities. Women, like men, will be given an opportunity to
access to the right kind of information on agricultural inputs. They will also
be given the opportunity of laearning and sharing best practices on appropriate
technologies (for example, improved drought-tolerant seeds, and integrated pest
management). Local inputs dealers will equally be involved to source
women-friendly means of production and technologies. The project’s focus on
cooperatives and women farmer groups structured around school meals programmes
can create a physical space where women can build their confidence to learn and
adopt new technologies, as well as gain negotiation and decision-making skills.
Equally important, the cooperative activities will enable women farmers to have
a new source of income independently from the household’s resources. Other areas
where the project can improve the adaptive capacities of women and men is making
weather and agriculture and market information readily available to both. The
project will ensure wide and inclusive dissemination of information on cropping
calendars, food prices, insurance, and micro-credit products etc.

 

 

C1. Increased dissemination of community-based risk preparedness measures
and use of climate information services and insurance products (risk
preparedness and risk transfer approach to address knowledge, regulatory and
institutional barriers)

The component will implement community-based adaptation measures to increase the
resilience of rural communities and restore the capacity of communal lands to
cope with climate change hazards. An estimated 70 villages will be targeted and
further identification and validation of areas and activities will be completed
during the development of the Funding Proposal and at project inception.
Following community and stakeholder consultations, the preliminary criteria for
selection of sites and activities included[2]
[https://wfp.sharepoint.com/sites/GCFCotedIvoire/Shared%20Documents/General/Concept%20Note%20development/Concept%20Note%20&%20attachments/4.%20Fourth%20submission%20to%20the%20GCF%20(December%202021)/GCF%20SAP-concept-note_Cote%20d'Ivoire_WFP_Revised_November2021_final.docx#_ftn2]:

 *  Incidence of climate impacts,
 *  Vulnerability and adaptive capacity of villagers
 *  Indicators of food and water insecurity
 *  Initiatives undertaken by WFP and/or other stakeholders
 *  The extent of degradation of communal lands
 *  Availablity of financial services
 *  Support from rural advisory and technical extension services
 *  Structure and functionality of FOs

 

Outcome 1. Smallholder communities are better informed and prepared to cope with
climate change hazards and threats

This outcome will leverage the dissemination of adaptation measures through
local development plans and the implementation of on-the-ground restoration
measures to enchance climate-resilience of rural population. They will also
increase their planning and decision-making and investment capacity to deal with
climate change impacts through improved access to climate information services
and insurance products. 




·      Activity 1.1 Integration of climate change adaptation and distaster risk
reduction measures in local development plans.

The activity will mainstream adaptation in communal plans to reduce
vulnerability through an integrated approach that will identify appropriate
measures and resources availability

·      Activity 1.2 Implementation of community-based adaptation/risk management
measures in vulnerable villages.

This activity will promote improved water management and water use efficiency as
well as enhancing and improving soil fertility, for example by training local
women and supporting them in taking leadership roles in management committees.
It will also support restoration of communal lands around the selected villages.
Climate-smart soil and water conservation and management will be implemented
through an integrated approach that will include crop rotation, fallow and
intercropping, soil cover improvement (mulch and plant cover), tillage systems
with minimum soil disturbance. Water conservation measures will also include
rainwater harvesting and improved water storage. Climate-smart land restoration
will include planting of forest and fruit trees, residue management and avoid
slash and burn and forest clearance based on measures identified in the adapted
development plans. Women will be specifically targeted, ensuring they have
access to information and decision-making.

·      Activity 1.3 Provide project beneficiaries with Index-based Insurance
products to mitigate climate change impacts of climate change hazards

The activity will support the development of index insurance products as risk
transfer mechanism for rural communities, focusing on smallholder producers,
protecting productive assets and leveraging livelihoods diversification options
implemented under activities 1.2, 2.2 and 2.3 against climate shocks. During
Funding Proposal development, a feasibility assessment will be carried out to
evaluate the conditions and requirements to establish and disseminate
sustainable insurance products based on previous experiences in the country and
taking into account WFP’s expertise with similar initiatives in West Africa.
Based on the preliminary assessment and consultations, WFP will establish the
micro-insurance scheme with selected FOs that would include also incentives for
its adoption, such as capacity-building, climate information services and a
subsided premium that will be phased out over the years.

·      Activity 1.4: Scale up the access and use of reliable, tailored and
timely climate information and weather forecasts

The activity will allow smallholder farmers and other end-users across the food
value chain to improve the access to tailored information for community-based
adaptive planning and decision-making. Climate information will be made
available through SMS, bulletins, leaflets, radio broadcasting sources
(depending on the most appropriate and preferred channel by women) to enhance
awareness on climate variability and tackle projected impacts of extreme weather
events and slow-onset events through weather forecasts. In particular, the
information will be shared in a way that will be suitable for women (for example
in case of illiteracy) and they will be capacitated in their understanding and
dissemination across communities.

 

C2. Increased smallholders’ adoption of climate-resilient practices and
technologies across the food value chains (risk reduction approach to address
technical, knowledge and gender barriers) 

This component focuses on increasing the adaptive capacity of small-scale
producers belonging to Farmers’ Organizations through climate-awareness,
capacity building and scaling up of adaptive technologies and approaches in
households’ farmlands.




Outcome 2. Smallholder producers improve and diversify food production and
reduce post-harvest losses.

Individual and groups of farmers will improve their productivity across the food
value chain by implementing nature-based agricultural practices and technologies
and from benefiting from technical assistance and climate information services
and products developed under component 1.

 

·      Activity 2.1: Awareness-raising, capacity-building and organizational
empowerment of smallholder farmers with focus on women and youths

The activity will support the capacity development of smallholder producers via
the realization of trainings (e.g. in-person courses, seminars, webinars,
e-learning); exchanges (e.g. peer-to-peer field visits, conferences, workshops,
sharing platforms, communities of practice); technical assistance (e.g. expert
missions, backstopping) focused on the implementation and scaling up of
climate-resilient measures in households’ farmlands. In addition,
awareness-raising campaigns will be included to leverage sensitization to target
households (farmers and vulnerable communities). This activity, conducted in
partnership with MINADER/ANADER, will provide the enabling environment for the
scaling up and dissemination of adaptive technologies and practices planned
under the activities 2.2 and 2.3. Adaptive technologies and practices will be
further assessed and described during the FP development stage with the
intention to systematize successful experiences and lessons learnt under
activity 3.3 (knowledge management)

·      Activity 2.2: Dissemination of adaptive nature-based agricultural
practices, technologies and approaches to increase and diversify staple food
production and improve soil and water conservation

Adaptive practices identified during preliminary consultations and project
prefeasibility phase include provision of quality seeds of drought-tolerant
varieties, implementation of conservation agriculture and agro-ecology
practices, agroforestry, biopesticides and compost artisanal production. The
approach for scaling up and dissemination of climate-resilient technologies will
include a combination of farmer field schools, model farms, demonstrative plots
and peer-to-peer exchange visits supported by extension and rural advisory to
farmers’ groups provided by ANADER and mostly run by women.

·      Activity 2.3 Improve processing and storage capacity to reduce
post-harvest losses due to climate shocks

This activity will improve the access of post-harvest processing and storage
technologies in favor of smallholder producers and actors across the food value
chain. Small-scale processing equipment will include mills for maize and rice
and solar driers for vegetables whereas silos and bags will be provided for
efficient storage.

 

C3. Smallholder FOs improve market access, set savings schemes and leverage
partnerships to strengthen the financial support across the food value chains
(prudent risk taking approach to address financial and gender barriers)

The component is supporting community and household-based ownership and
sustainability of the adaptive measures through increased financial and market
access and knowledge dissemination. Basically, the component ensures that
climate-proofing investments implemented respectively by communities and farm
households under components 1 and 2 are sustained and spread across the Poro
Region.




Outcome 3. Smallholders improve the financial sustainability of their food
production systems

Climate adaptation measures are disseminated and continued after the projects
thanks to the improved access to saving and loans mechanisms and
complementarities generated with other resilience initiatives and enhanced
market alliances.

 

·      Activity 3.1. Support FOs to develop business plans and community-based
saving and loans mechanisms for climate-proofing investments

This activity will focus on improving the financial literacy of members of FOs,
and support the development of climate-resilient business plans to ensure
complementarity with other climate-resilient initiatives under design, for
example, the IGREENFIN Funding Proposal that falls under the GCF Great Green
Wall Umbrella Programme. The establishment of Village-based Saving and Loans
Associations (VSLA) will be another critical element to protect and incentivize
climate-proofing investments and provide seed money for the implementation of
business plans, therefore enhancing financial eligibility and complementarities
with the IGREENFIN FP under design. Few options to ensure viability of the FOs
will be analyzed and selected for the business plans. The linkages with
IGREENFIN will be one of these, given the ongoing joint discussions on the
design of the IFAD and WFP’s projects to promote funding options to FOs.
However, other sources of livelihoods will be included, such as improved incomes
from local/national market sales and school canteens. These latter are sources
of incomes already established by WFP and will be scaled out through improved
market information and commercialization of targeted project crops, in
particular off-season vegetables. Finally, the establishment of VSLA, already
proved successful in Western Africa will allow better efficiencies across the
food value chain through for example the purchase of adapted inputs,
agricultural products and tools for short-cycle crops which is the case for the
selected project crops (maize, rice and vegetables). The resources provided to
the VSLA will derive from the contribution of members of FOs that will improve
the sales of agricultural products through adaptive crop production and improved
market linkages. This will also build from existing local saving mechanisms,
such as village-based loans or shared funds subscribed by a group of
beneficiaries for a certain duration (‘’tontines’’).

 

·      Activity 3.2 Build farm to market alliances in particular between FOs and
school canteens and local markets

This activity will strengthen the capacity of targeted women smallholder
producers to sell farm products through various options including improved
linkages with school canteens and local markets. Moreover, the activity will
strengthen capacities of public and private structures involved in food product
commercialization with support of technology and IT equipment.

WFP has already established a channel to disseminate of market information
through radio broadcasting and enhance partnerships with FOs under the school
feeding program implemented across the country. This activity will exploit the
potential of the market information services by including all the FOs covered by
the project, facilitating producer-buyers exchanges and promoting alliances
across the selected food value chains (maize, rice and vegetables) with local
suppliers and intermediaries

·      Activity 3.3 Systematization of good practices and knowledge-sharing of
adaptive solutions across food value chains

This activity will collect and systematize successful experiences and lessons
learnt related to adaptive technologies and good practices that have been
implemented under activities 1.2, 1.3, 2.2 and 2.3. The deliverable of this
activity will be a technical compendium of adaptation measures that will be
shared with stakeholders with the intention to expand the dissemination and
institutionalize the practices into local and national policies and plans.

 

3.    WFP has been involved in resilience building in the project identified
regions over the past 5 years. Much has been learned about sociocultural context
in which the project will be implemented. WFP starts with strong advocacy which
usually involves the participation of existing sociocultural and administrative
structures in place in these communities. Community leaders play a crucial role
in the buy-in of WFP’s proposed model of linking women farmer groups to local
school meals programmes as the ultimate reason for the support to the
smallholders. Land donation is a very participatory process that comes with
sufficient safeguards from within each beneficiary communities. There exist
various products from the proposed project that will meet the needs of both men
and women it is designed to deliver activities that are participatory and
converging in nature. This means both men and women will participate in
productive assets’ restoration and regeneration and protection. The project
delivery mechanism, messaging, advocacy will put emphasis on community
resilience rather than the welfare of selected groups.

 

4.    The transformational potential of the project revolves around
strengthening the adaptive capacity and empowering smallholder farmers, in
particular women and youths, who represent about 90 percent of direct
beneficiaries, to reduce their vulnerability to climate shocks and sustainably
improve the resilience of food production systems. The transformational and
incremental distinction of the project consists in streamlining gender-sensitive
actions for climate empowerment in an integrated manner. The project mainstreams
an integrated community-based risk management approach: Risk reduction, risk
preparedness, risk transfer, risk retention and prudent risk-taking that builds
upon the R4 Programme successfully implemented by WFP in other areas of the
continent, including West Africa.




5.    The innovative elements of this project consists in strengthening the
technical and financial capacity of women smallholder producers in order to:




·      Mainstream climate risk and adaptive management practices through climate
and market information,

·      Enhance awareness and knowledge and promote the dissemination of
climate-proofing technologies across priority food value chains,

·      Introduce index-based micro-insurance products and strengthen financial
inclusion of farmers in Poro Region as a way to promote climate-smart
investments and protect productive assets,

·      Leverage effective organizational capacity and women empowerment,

·      Promote linkages and complementarities with other GCF Great Green Wall
Umbrella Programme Initiatives, for example the IGREENFIN FP under formulation
by strengthening the capacity of FOs to become self-sustaining through the
development of business plans, and

·      Support the establishment of market alliances between FOs and private
actors to ensure the sustainability of the activities after the project.




In addition, the project will support the application of national adaptation
strategies and plans at community and household levels by mainstreaming and
implementing climate-resilient measures such as soil and water conservation,
micro-watershed land restoration, adapted and sustainable staple crop production
as part of the integrated livelihoods risk reduction strategy. Eventually, FOs
in particular women and youths associations will be able to increase and
diversify food supply to school feeding programmes and local markets through
enhanced production, improved processing and storage, and better access to
climate, financial and market services which will ensure viability and
sustainaibility of project interventions.

security and livelihoods’ resilience, reduce malnutrition, enhance education and
enhance gender equality. The project is aligned and contributes to the Strategic
Outcome 4 of WFP’s 2019-2023 Country Strategic Plan: “Enhance food systems to be
more sustainable and resilient”. Upon the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, WFP
has introduced protective measures in its operations in line with Government´s
guidelines[3]

activities including baseline data collection, field surveys, consultations with
beneficiaries, preparation and follow-up of the annual work plan and budget, and

Committee will meet twice a year to monitor achievement of project milestones
and ensure that objectives are being accomplished.

9.    Financial Risks:

·      Financial services providers see smallholder farmers as high-risk
investments (medium) in agricultural activities. Mitigation: The project will
work with farmers’ organizations that will be supported and empowered through
the project implementation. Financial providers will be selected during project
pre-feasibility on the basis of their successful experiences in the Poro Region
with producer’s organizations. In addition, village savings and loans groups
will be the basis for financial inclusion and financial risk management at
community level.

·      Financial sustainability of climate-proofed investments (high).
Mitigation: Organizations will develop climate-smart business plans, get access
to savings and loans and complementarities with other climate-resilience
initiatives.

·      Basis risk challenges with the index-based Insurance product (medium).
Mitigation: WFP will work with expert providers and in-house specialists to
minimize such risk during product development and ensure indexes are adequately
set and regularly updated. Crowd sourcing technology or area yield indices
(based on crop cuts experiments) are 2 ways of lowering basis risk.

·      Coverage and payment of insurance premiums (medium). Mitigation: The
project will establish 2 mechanisms to address this risk: 1) savings and loans
associations to protect protective assets and incentivize climate-proofing
investments, and 2) improved yields and farmer household’s revenues expected
from the implementation of adaptive technologies will be able to cover insurance
premiums

 

10.    Information gaps Risks:

Farmers do not have access or use climate information and weather forecasts
provided by the project to adequately plan farm works and calendars (medium):
Government and donor-funded initiative support the appropriate functioning of
early warning system/hydromet network across the country. The project will
catalyze the linkages between the information available and the dissemination to
final users and build technical capacity for sustained use of climate
information in farm management decision-making.

 

11.    Operational Risks:

·      Land tenure rights and conflicts linked to site interventions including
transhumance (medium): the Project will conduct, prior to any site intervention,
a community-based social and environmental screening. Also, the project will
conduct a participatory planning exercise of the priority adaptive measures and
mitigation options to avoid community conflicts over land tenure rights between
farmers and transhumant herders. The community and municipal development plans
will be amended to integrate adaptive measures through a participatory approach
that will include the key stakeholders including community leaders, herders, and
landowners. The ownership and management of productive assets, in particular
land and water resources, will be defined at community level and monitored
through the project implementation. Project activities, for example
climate-smart land restoration, rehabilitation of storing facilities, soil
conservation structures, etc. will be conditional to the allocation and
contribution of community members (including both herders and farmers) and
monitored by project team and community leaders. Women will be prioritized based
on the gender analysis and action plan to reduce side-effects related to
gender-based violence and women discrimination.

·      Communities reluctant to adopt climate-resilient agriculture practices
(low): Activities and approaches that will be utilized for the adoption and
dissemination of climate-resilient agriculture practices and technologies in
food production systems will include awareness-raising, trainings, peer-to-peer
experience sharing, climate-resilient model farms and farmer field schools,
access to microcredits and insurance for the uptake of climate-proofing
investments.

·      Insecurity due to political instability and terrorism (medium): The
project will develop security and safety operation guidelines for personnel and
partners. Likelihood of terrorist attacks in the project area is low.

·      Limited operationality due to the COVID-19 pandemic public health
restrictions (medium): It is highly likely that by the project start date, the
pandemic could be under control. However, the project will closely monitor its
evolution in close coordination with health authorities; all precautionary
measures will be adopted to prevent the spread of the disease and mitigate the
risk of low operationality in the area.

48.) 1. The project objective is to enhance the efficiency and coverage of
multi-hazard early warning system for climate change induced hazards in
Uzbekistan in view of the projected climate change impacts. The approach
combines principles articulated in the Global Framework for Climate Services
(GFCS) with a ‘value-chain’ approach to target specific weaknesses in the
delivery of early warning services, given the specific modes of operation,
current infrastructure, technical capacities and institutional arrangements in
Uzbekistan. The project will introduce the impact-based MHEWS based on the
socio-economic risk modelling and will explore and facilitate elements of
forecast-based financing as an innovative paradigm-shifting approach to the use
of climate data in decision-making. In particular, the project will:




a. Improve methods and data/models used to monitor and forecast variables needed
to derive climate characteristics;

b. Develop the capacity of national agencies to model climate-related hazards
(hydrological drought, landslides, mudflows and avalanches) and to utilize
modern weather and seasonal forecasting techniques;

c. Expand areas and geophysical/biophysical observations using satellite-based
remote sensing (including the monitoring of precipitation, vegetation, snow
cover and landslip/slides) to monitor and assess hazard risks over extensive
regions of Uzbekistan, especially those regions where it is impractical to place
observational equipment; 

d. Introduce a socio-economic risk and vulnerability modelling as an integral
element of the impact-based multi-hazard EWS;

e. Create a central repository/facility incorporating an advanced information
management system for the management, forecasting and monitoring of
hydrometeorological processes; 

f. Enhance the regulatory framework, coordination and institutional mechanisms
for an effective impact-based MHEWS and promote better regional cooperation for
managing transboundary risks through existing and new regional coordination
platforms; and

g. Strengthen the “last mile” delivery of disaster-related communication and
interaction with end users, in particular those in the communities with highest
risks in Uzbekistan. 




49.) In order to transform the current EWS in Uzbekistan from a reactive system
to one based on preventive warnings ahead of an event, it is necessary to: 

i) improve the efficiency in collecting and generating/forecasting weather and
climate information; and

ii) develop methods and operational systems which translate weather/climate
information/forecasts into actionable warnings and disseminate them to users who
understand their content and how best to react. 




50.)Output 1 will address the first element by investing in automatic
hydro-meterological monitoring infrastructure required for the generation of
hazard-specific forecasting and risk models. Output 2 and Output 3 focus on the
second aspect, building the systems and modelling capacity to generate
impact-based forecasts[1] [https://eforms.gcfund.org/#_ftn1],[2]
[https://eforms.gcfund.org/#_ftn2], creating dissemination channels to first
responders and communities through updated communication technologies to enable
real-time risk analysis and evaluation, as well as working with communities at
risk to be able to interpret, understand and react to those warnings. 




51.) .Building upon the feasibility analysis, this proposed project will address
risks of avalanches, flooding, droughts, landslides, and mudflows. This project
is designed to enhance the capability of detecting, monitoring, analysis and
forecasting of these climate hazards in Uzbekistan, and develop impact-based
risk knowledge products to enable warning dissemination and forecast-based
actions in targeted areas. More specifically, the project has identified 15
districts located in seven provinces in eastern Uzbekistan as hazard-prone
target regions. They are: Qoichirchik, Bostanlik, Sirdarya, Saihunabad, S.
Rashidov, Gallaaral, Bulungur, Jambai, Koshrabad, Kitab, Yakkabag, Dehkanabad,
Chust, Turakurgan, and Dangarin. The establishment of an effective MHEWS will
allow the creation of geographically explicit risk maps with vulnerablity
assessment on population, assets and infrastructure in target regions. Business
sectors such as aviation, transport, agriculture (water irrigation services)
will likely benefit from improved hazard forecasting and alerting services to
prevent potential disruption of services, delays and loss of agricultural
productivity and incomes in target regions.

Output 1: Upgraded hydro-meteorological observation network, modelling and
forecasting capacities

52.)The proposed intervention will create a more efficient monitoring network
for weather, climate, hydrology and cryosphere, through both upgrading existing
(automating) and installing new monitoring equipment (automatic weather stations
(AWS), automatic hydrological stations, upper air sounding stations, and
strategically placed low cost radars. This equipment and other existing data
streams will be integrated into high availability/redundant single databases.
Hazard-specific forecasting procedures will be developed and operationalized for
climate-induced hazards. Training of Uzhydromet staff to undertake forecasting, 
operation and maintenance and data QA/QC/archiving procedures will also
accompany these activities. Activities follow the GFCS and in this output are
designed to address aspects related to: i) observations and monitoring; and ii)
research, modelling and prediction:




53.)Activity 1.1 Upgrading and modernization of the meteorological and
hydrological Observation System. This will include upgrading/automation of 25
meteorological observation stations and equipment (software, workstations etc),
modernizing the ground-based infrastructure (telemetry processing, hydrogen
generators etc) for 2 upper-air stations (Uzhydromet/GoU will support the
establishment of 2 more), installing 2 online X-band doppler radar systems to
cover current gaps in mountainous areas, upgrading and technical equipment of 90
hydrological stations , and establishing benchmarks and up to date equipment for
instrument calibration (vacuum chambers, mobile laboratory etc). AWS and
hydrological stations will be installed/upgraded at existing facilities and
premises of key locations in the mountains above hazardous valleys and in the
areas of high precipitation/landslides/mudflow risks, not already covered by
investments through the CACILM and CAMP4ASB projects, as shown in Figure 46
(page 66) of the FS. All equipment will report data to central servers at
Uzhydromet and will conform to WMO standards, including reporting to the Global
Climate Observing System (GCOS), Global Basic Observing Network (GBON) and
Global Telecommunication System (GTS). The project will also assist the
government to identify long-term requirements and to enable budgeting and
planning for the maintenance of all observing systems. 







54.)Activity 1.2 Upgrading Uzhydromet’s capacity to store, process and develop
hazard products, as well as to communicate hydrometeorological data to regional
divisions. This is a climate services information system (as described in GFCS)
and involves the establishment of an operations centre, ICT servers and
networking equipment to integrate data streams (hydrometeorological and
satellite-based observations) and automate processes and analyses (including
hazard forecasts). Software and processing routines will enable data and maps to
be exported in common formats for sharing with partners and importing into the
MES risk management system (see activity 2.1 below). A local cloud-based
solution will be implemented to store and manage data that will benefit from
offsite backups and easier access for the MES risk management system. 
Specifically this activity will: i) Integrate hydrometeorological data (from
both automatic and manually operated stations) into a single database as a basis
for developing products based on all available observed data. Automatically
transmitted data from different providers/manufacturers will be integrated and
undergo quality control/assurance within a single database in real time and will
be available for interrogation via geo-visualization software. This activity
will also: i) Expand the hydrological drought early warning system for Amu Darya
(developed by the UNDP/AF project) to the Syr Darya and Zeravshon rivers. All
historical streamflow and flood data for the two rivers will be collected and
forecast models, with data ingestion and data processing routines, will be
derived; ii) Develop automatic procedures for calculating avalanche risk in real
time. Software and code will be developed to automatically update avalanche
hazard maps based on snow accumulation from satellites (and AWS) and established
procedures for estimating avalanche extent; iii) Develop code and procedures for
automatically calculating mudflow risk maps based on precipitation observations
and forecasts for 2-3 days lead time; iv) Develop a landslide risk model for
Eastern Uzbekistan based on geophysical and geotechnical characteristics,
including subsurface water and extreme rainfall. The skill of all developed
forecast systems will be assessed using retroactive forecasts and used to assess
their utility for forecast based actions in activity 2.1 and 2.2.







55.)Activity 1.3 Re-training and advanced training of Uzhydromet staff on
monitoring and forecasting technologies and procedures (training of MES and
geotechnical staff is covered in output 2 below). International experts will
train weather forecasters to work with new products of the KOSMO model (with a
resolution of 13 km and 2 km). Refresher courses and advanced training will be
provided for new software and equipment, including the introduction of new
methods for the analysis and prediction of hydrometeorologically important
variables and climate hazards. The project will facilitate organization of
on-the-job trainings, engagement with universities, courses and seminars with
the involvement of foreign specialists. Training of IT specialists of Uzhydromet
will be conducted for work with the computer center and operation of the KOSMO
model, the UNIMAS, MITRA information reception and transmission system,
workstation software (for weather forecasters, agrometeorologists, GIS-METEO,
etc.) and EU Copernicus programme on satellite data, all of which will be used
for impact-based forecasting where needed . Trainings on AWS installation,
general user training and technical support will be provided. These increased
capacities will also assist Uzhydromet in fulfilling its regional role as a WMO
RMSC, in accordance with the GFCS capacity development, and help improve their
capacity for regional cooperation.




Output 2: Output 2: A functional impact-based Multi-Hazard Early Warning System
is established based on innovative impact modelling, risk analyses, effective
regional communication and community awareness



56.) The proposed intervention will integrate and develop ICT systems to use the
hydro-meteorological hazards predicted in output 1, and combine these with
vulnerability data to identify risks and provide information for planning and
mitigating their impacts. It will improve the efficiency of the current early
warning system by automating the sharing and production of risk-related data, as
well as the communication of warnings. The project will also develop
methodologies for and support hazard and risk mapping and risk zoning for key
climate-induced hazards (floods, landslides, mudflows, droughts and avalanche).
Specifically it will introduce an advanced, impact-based information management
system for combining data on socio-economics (population, livelihoods, poverty
indicators), infrastructure (roads, utilities, buildings, bridges etc) and the
natural environment (landcover, vegetation, soils etc) in order to operationally
assess the risks associated with each hazard forecast. This information will be
transmitted and shared with RCMCs in key hazard-prone districts in Uzbekistan so
that regional teams have the most up to date information available for planning
their operations. Building on the existing mobile-based public dissemination
platforms, the project will develop geographically specific risk based warnings
tailored to the areas affected by each hazard (e.g. mudflows, avalanches,
landslides and flooding). Based on the user interaction guideline of GFCS,
inputs from consulations with key stakeholders and end-users (activities 3.1 and
3.3) will inform the design and dissemination of warnings and alerts to
communities at risk. The following specific activities are proposed:




57.)Activity 2.1 Developing and installing a modernised and efficient system for
assessing climate risks based on dynamic information on both hazards and
vulnerabilities, including socio-economic risk models for decision making and
prioritization of resilience building long-term/future investments. This would
enable establishing an impact-based MHEWS, where hazard forecasting is linked to
the risk and exposure information (socio-economic risk model).This involves
installing both hardware and software to enable an advanced, impact-based
information management system to be built, which will combine data on current
vulnerabilities (e.g. indicators of poverty, education, health, housing etc),
public and private assets (including infrastructure, roads, railways, housing,
mines, airports, hospitals, schools etc), the environment (crops, lakes, rivers,
tourism areas etc) and hazard impacts (input from Output 1) to operationally
assess the risks associated with each hazard forecast. Based on evaluated risks
and the skill of each impact-based forecast, a set of feasible ex-ante actions
will be identified for different lead times. This activity will also develop
software and standard operating procedures to automatically ingest hydrological
and meteorological observations, weather and seasonal forecasts, and derived
drought/avalanche/mudflow/landslide forecasts from Uzhydromet (through activity
1.2) into the system to be combined with available vulnerability data. The
system will also import long-term climate change scenarios to be used for
forward planning and evaluation of future risks.

58.)Activity 2.2 Developing and introducing technical guidance, institutional
and coordination frameworks to increase the efficiency of: i) data collection
and archiving (activities 1.1 and 1.2); ii) hazard mapping and modelling
(activity 1.2); iii) risk assessment (activity 2.1); iv) impact-based warning
and forecast-based actions (activity 3.2); and v) dissemination of information
to RCMCs (activity 2.3). These protocols are also required to ensure that new
climate information sources (e.g. AWS, AWLS, radar and satellite observations –
activity 1.1) are translated into products that are useful for decision making 
and investment by MES and Uzhydromet (based on feedback obtained through

Guidance and procedures will be developed to support the application of
socio-economic risk models and enhanced risk zoning in development planning and
decision-making (activity 2.1).







59.)Activity 2.3 Designing and implementing a system for information
dissemination to RCMCs and area specific mobile alerts including an information
visualization system for RCMCs with software. This involves setting up
information visualisation and analysis systems (video walls, telecommunication
systems, servers and ICT storage) at 7 RCMS, to enable them to visualise the
maps and impact forecast information provided through the risk analysis and
warning system (activity 2.1) and combine it with local (regionally available)
information on current vulnerabilities and field-based information. This will
enable them to better target advice and direct regional response teams. This
activity will further develop (improving the existing MES dissemination system)
area-specific mobile and SMS based warnings for mudflows, avalanches, landslides
and flooding. This will reduce the chance of false alarms sent to those not at
risk, as well as improve the content based on information from the improved MES
risk and impact-based forecast system (activity 2.1 and 2.2). Inputs from
consulations with key stakeholders and end-users (activities 3.1 and 3.3) will
be used to design the dissemination system, following the co-design and
co-production user interaction guideline of GFCS. 




Output 3: Strengthened climate services and disaster communication to end users




60.) The proposed intervention will strengthen the effectiveness of delivering
climate information services and disaster warnings to users in Uzbekistan at two
levels. On the overall national level, the project will initiate the
establishment of the National Framework of Climate Services as a mechanism to
systematically bring together producers and users of hydrometeorological and
climate information and to ensure that information and services reach their end
recipients both in the various sectors of the government and the society and at
the different geographic levels down to local communities. Disaster-related
information and services being the specific focus of the project, it will work
with the various public and private stakeholders to reorient the existing
financial / economic model behind the provision of such services to make it more
cost-efficient and sustainable in the long-term, i.a. using private investment
and partnership opportunities on the domestic and the international markets.
Finally, on the warning dissemination and communication aspectconcrete project
level, updated communication technolgoies a range of options will be utilised to
support real-time risk evaluation by Regional disaster managemen agencies
(RCMCs) and first responders and ensure ‘last-mile’ delivery of early warning
risk information to the communities at risk and population at large. In
collaboration with Red Crescent Society and other community-level NGOs, RCMC
will organize trainings and annual community forums to help communities at risk
better interpret, understand and react to those warnings, as well as facilitate
forecast-based actions and responsesensure The following specific activities are
proposed:




61.)Activity 3.1Establishing National Framework for Climate Services for
Uzbekistan

The Global Framework for Climate Services (GFCS), promoted and facilitated by
the World Meteorological Organization in cooperation with GFCS partner
organisations, is a framework that envisions better risk management and more
efficient adaptation to climate variability and change through improvements in
the quality, delivery and use of climate-related information in planning, policy
and practice. GFCS, i.a. endorsed by the GCF Climate Services Strategy, focuses
on developing and delivering information services in agriculture and food
security, disaster risk reduction, energy, health and water, and organises its
work around observations and monitoring; climate services information systems;
research, modelling and pre- diction; user interface platforms; and capacity
development. A strong focus of GFCS is on a multi-stakeholder approach to the
definition and the actual delivery of services, thus bringing users and
co-producers of climate and hydrometeorological information together and to the
centre of the design and production process as opposed to more traditional
supply-driven approaches. 




62.)Following an accepted WMO blueprint for the conceptualising and
establishment of a NFCS, the project will undertake a baseline assessment of
climate services in Uzbekistan, followed by multi-stakeholder consultations and
the participatory development of the country's NFCS concept and Action Plan to
be endorsed both by stakeholders and at the high executive level, ready for
implementation once supplementary NFCS-earmarked funds become available as a
follow-up to the project.




63.)As part of this activity, a platform will be set up to engage end users in
the design and testing of new disaster-related climate information services and
products. Similarly, a National Climate Outlook Forum will be established and
supported as one mechanism to help shape and deliver climate services with
longer time horizon, i.a. with a particular focus on disasters such as
hydrological droughts. A connection will then established between the Forum and
WMO’s Regional Climate Fora operating in Europe (NEACOF) as well as Asia
(FOCRAII).




64.) Activity 3.2Designing sustainable business model for disaster-related
information and services

While it may not be realistic to expect any significant level of private
financing during project implementation given the existing public service
management model and the time required for transition, there is long-term
potential for private sector investment in climate information services and for
expanded service provision to private sector based on enhanced
hydrometeorological and climate information in Uzbekistan, including those
related to natural disasters and early warning. Linked to the NFCS process
above, the project will conduct a comprehensive analysis and discussion of
long-term sustainable financing options for disaster-related services in
Uzbekistan beyond current state-funding model, in particular drawing on blended
finance through dedicated national funds and public-private partnership
opportunities.This will include seeking financing, from both public and private
sources, for forecast based (ex-ante) actions identified in activities 2.1 and
2.2. Based on the analysis and consultations, a sustainable value chain-based
business model for disaster-related information will be developed and agreed
with the key stakeholders, and the necessary legal and organisation changes will
be outlined and planned on the national (adjustment of legislation) and the
inter-institutional levels (Uzhydromet, Ministry of Emergency Situations, users
of the services, private investors).




65.)Activity 3.3 Strengthening disaster warning dissemination and communication 
with end users.

The project will significantly strengthen interaction with the end users with
the aim to communicate and facilitate proactive responses to disaster
information and warnings in Uzbekistan. Within the 15 RCMCs, outdoor
communication boards will be set up in identified communities at highest risk to
alert and inform the population in real time about threats or emergencies,
following which, through cooperation between MES RCMCs and the Red Crescent
Society, communities will be trained to interpret and use information on climate
hazards and early warnings. Printed visual information (leaflets) will be
provided to RCMCs and Uzbekistan’s communities on climate hazards and associated
early warnings. With expected increase of user interaction level, regional staff
of MES RCMCs will be further trained in the effective use of this information to
support community interactions (crowd sourcing and survey data) and formulate
forecast-based actions following the guidelines developed in Activity 2.2.
Similarly, easy-to-understand and visual information will be channelled to mass
media through existing agreements between them and MES / Uzhydromet, as well as
to national NGOs. Finally, this activity will also complement the prior Activity
2.3 in the development of region-specific (as opposed to the currently used
national-wide) broadcasting of early warnings, with the use of other modern
communication channels such as social media and electronic messenger
subscription groups. In addition, the project will establish a platform for
organizing annual community forums on community-based EWS engaging target
communities and representatives of vulnerable groups to exchange information,
lessons learned, successes and opportunities. Through such platforms regular
competitions will be organized engaging both youth and the most active community
representative to advocate for structural and non-structure mesures and ensure
their inclusiveness.

1. The paradigm shift objective is to enhance climate resilience of livelihoods
and agro-sylvo-pastoral ecosystem services, while reducing greenhouse gas
emissions from land use in Kordofan, Sudan.[6] The restoration of agroforestry
systems with gum arabic trees will protect the annual crops against increased
moisture stress, improving the resilience of the livelihoods of poor smallholder
farmers (Component 1). By building the capacity of 500 smallholder gum arabic
producer associations (GAPAs) to produce larger quantities of clean, dry gum and
facilitating contract farming arrangements (Component 1), the project will
mobilize private sector financing for these GAPAs.[7] Gum companies such as
Elemats[8], confident that GAPAs will be able to deliver on their high-quality
gum delivery commitments, will provide GAPAs with pre-financing and key inputs
(such as jute bags) to increase quantity and quality of gum production and will
pay them a price premium for clean, dry gum.[9] Once smallholder groups will
have “graduated” from the project’s capacity building efforts, they will be able
to continue to leverage the purchase guarantees of the gum exporters mobilized
by the project to obtain formal financial services from microfinance
institutions – including from Ebda’a Microfinance Bank, which has already
committed to provide additional credit for smallholder gum producers in
partnership with the project (using the exporting companies’ purchase guarantees
as collateral).[10] GAPAs will also be able to invest in their own storage
facilities, allowing them to sell some of the gum later in the season, when the
prices are higher, through the newly established higher grade and standard for
quality gum at the public auction. These economic benefits will provide a strong
incentive, inducing beneficiaries to continue to invest in and maintain the
restored landscapes producing these benefits, thereby establishing a reinforcing
and virtuous cycle of climate change adaptation and carbon sequestration through
poverty reduction. Component 2 invests in livestock mobility and rangeland
restoration, which improves the resilience of pastoralists to climate shocks and
takes the pressure off the gum agroforestry systems supported under Component 1
– and thereby generates further land use emissions reductions.

8.Component 1 outputs and activities: Component 1 is “Restoration of smallholder
gum agroforestry systems, reforestation of degraded land and improvement of
smallholder gum value chains”. This component has two outputs (1.1 and 1.2) that
generate direct greenhouse gas mitigation benefits through gum agroforestry
restoration and reforestation activities, and four outputs (1.3 to 1.6) that
focus on adaptation. Under the adaptation outputs, the project will build the
capacity of 500 smallholder gum Arabic producer groups (GAPAs) to produce
sizable quantities of clean, dry gum – as gum trees are by far the most
resilient crop with regards to tolerating increasing moisture stress in the
project area – and reposition them in the value chain and link them up with
financial services providers. The doubling of gum producer prices, achieved
under the AFD-funded pilot project that GAMS will scale-up, not only immediately
improves farm household resilience to climate change shocks, but also promotes
longer-term adaptation as the promoted gum agroforestry systems are more
resilient to the projected climate change stresses, and creates incentives for
smallholders to maintain carbon sequestration (mitigation) results over time.
The GAPAs to benefit from component 1 would be selected by the project on the
basis of the following two criteria: (i) Willingness to engage actively in gum
agroforestry restoration (output 1.1) or reforestation (output 1.2) activities,
which is essential as the GAPA members would need to devote significant labour
(and farm land for output 1.1)[12]; and (ii) willingness to apply the
accountability and transparency tools developed under the AFD-funded pilot
project (see appendix c of Chapter 6 of Annex 13, PFS) that GAMS aims to scale
up, in order to guarantee that benefits accruing to GAPAs are shared equitably
with the GAPA members. This component has six outputs and 14 associated
activities, as follows:




9.Output 1.1 and associated activities: Output 1.1 is “75,000 hectares of gum
agroforestry systems (40,000 ha in West Kordofan; 35,000 ha in North Kordofan)
restored by local communities supported by the project”. While the restoration
targets are large numbers, they will be achieved through bottom-up planning and
a Community-Driven Development Approach, with site selection dictated by the
preferences of the GAPA members making the land available for restoration. Based
on FNC’s recent experience with different plantation establishment techniques in
North and West Kordofan, direct seeding of tree seeds together with crop seeds
will be the preferred technique.[13] This may be supported by mechanical soil
preparation where necessary. To support these restoration activities, FNC will
use hashab seed from its own stocks, or procure it locally from women’s groups
and farmers. It may also obtain quality seed from the Forestry Research Centre
(striking a balance between gum yield and resilience to moisture stress, and
thus facilitating climate change adaptation). Where necessary, it may engage
local machine operators in soil preparation. Where direct seeding is not
feasible, FNC may procure seedlings from local women’s groups’ nurseries or use
seedlings from its own nurseries.




10. The project will provide technical support for women to become tree
seed/seedling suppliers in the restoration process based on their effective
skills (household/village tree nursery production, production of seedlings,
collection of improved seeds, transportation of seedlings, and providing
technical support). The achievement of this output will be verified using data
provided by Locality teams (LT) and state coordination units (SCU) including a
georeferenced M&E Archive and reports. Changes in Land productivity dynamics
(LPD) and Normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) will be assessed against

and 1.1.4. In the generation of this output, a different implementation approach
will be followed for those communities (referred to as “phase one”) that already
have completed Village Cluster Level Adaptation Plans (VCLAP), including
restoration targets, and those that do not (“phase two communities”), as
detailed below.
